era_context: Late Bronze Age, Egypt under Ramesses II, Hittite Empire under Muwatalli II. Mycenaean Greece in Late Helladic IIIB. Shang Dynasty in China. This is the era of great imperial powers and international diplomacy. The Late Bronze Age collapse is still several decades away, but the era is characterized by military competition and complex international relations.

events.title: Late Bronze Age international trade networks at their height
events.region: Eastern Mediterranean, Near East, Egypt, Levant
events.description: International trade networks connecting Egypt, the Hittite Empire, Mycenaean Greece, Phoenicia, and the Levant are at their height. Goods, ideas, and people move across the Mediterranean and Near East. Mycenaean pottery, Hittite metalwork, Egyptian luxury goods, and Levantine cedar are exchanged in a complex web of commercial relationships. This period represents the apex of Bronze Age international commerce before the Late Bronze Age collapse.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence of trade goods in multiple regions
Egyptian and Hittite administrative records mentioning trade

events.certainty_note: International trade networks are archaeologically confirmed. The specific year assignment is approximate within the Late Bronze Age.

events.title: Egyptian-Hittite military confrontation and diplomatic maneuvering
events.description: Ramesses II and Muwatalli II engage in military confrontation and diplomatic maneuvering for control of Syria and the Levant. Both powers maintain large armies and conduct military campaigns. The exact nature and outcome of specific military engagements in this year are uncertain, but both powers are actively competing for territorial control and influence. This period represents the height of Egyptian-Hittite military competition.

sources.name: Egyptian temple reliefs and administrative records
Hittite royal inscriptions and clay tablets

events.certainty_note: Egyptian-Hittite military competition is well-documented, but precise year-by-year dating is uncertain (±3–5 years).

events.title: Mycenaean palace civilization at its height
events.description: Mycenaean palace centers (Mycenae, Tiryns, Pylos) are at the height of their power and influence. These palatial centers control extensive territories and maintain sophisticated administrative systems. Linear B tablets document the organization of labor, commodity distribution, and religious practices. Mycenaean culture is at its most developed, with advanced architecture, art, and literature (though the latter is not yet written in the Greek alphabet).

sources.name: Linear B tablets from Pylos, Mycenae, and Knossos
Archaeological evidence of palace architecture and artifacts

events.certainty_note: Mycenaean palace civilization is directly attested in Linear B tablets and archaeological remains. The specific year assignment is approximate within Late Helladic IIIB.

events.title: Shang Dynasty at the height of its power
events.region: China (Yellow River valley and surrounding regions)
events.description: The Shang Dynasty is at the height of its power and territorial control. The Shang state maintains control over a large network of vassal states and conducts regular military campaigns to secure resources and maintain political authority. Oracle bones and bronze vessels document the sophistication of Shang administration, religion, and military organization. This period represents the mature phase of Shang civilization before its eventual decline.

sources.name: Oracle bones and bronze vessels from archaeological sites

events.certainty_note: Shang political organization is archaeologically confirmed. Precise year dating within the Shang Dynasty remains uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The characterization of this period as the 'height' of various civilizations is somewhat teleological, as it is based on knowledge of the Late Bronze Age collapse that follows. Contemporary sources do not indicate awareness of impending collapse. Additionally, the dating of specific events within this period is uncertain by ±3–5 years. The Battle of Kadesh, often associated with this era, is traditionally dated to c. 1274 BCE, but some scholars argue for earlier or later dates. The Shang Dynasty's exact chronology remains disputed among Chinese historians.
historiographic_note: This year's record is dominated by sources from literate imperial powers. Egyptian and Hittite sources present official narratives that may exaggerate military successes. Mycenaean sources are limited to administrative tablets and archaeological remains. Chinese sources are abundant but compiled much later. The vast majority of human societies in this year are invisible in the historical record. The sources available for this period are heavily weighted toward the eastern Mediterranean and Near East, with limited information from other regions.

graph_edges.note: International trade networks and military competition occur simultaneously
Egyptian-Hittite military competition occurs while Mycenaean civilization is at its height
Mycenaean palace civilization eventually collapses during the Late Bronze Age collapse
